coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous electrochemical determination of hydroquinone, catechol and resorcinol at Nafion/multi-walled carbon nanotubes/carbon dots/multi-walled carbon nanotubes modified glassy carbon electrode
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Chan
Huang, Qitong
Hu, Shirong
Zhang, Hanqiang
Zhang, Wuxiang
Wang, Zhaoming
Zhu, Menglin
Dai, Pingwang
Huang, Lizhang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, an excellent electrochemical sensor based on Nafion/multi-walled carbon nanotubes/carbon dots/multi-walled carbon nanotubes (Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs) for simultaneous determination of hydroquinone (HQ), catechol (CC) and resorcinol (RS) in 0.1M phosphate buffer solutions (PBS, pH=7.0) was found. The functioned MWCNTs, which contained amido bond, were combined to CDs via the electrostatic interaction to build the Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs modified electrode. As we know, MWCNTs have large specific surface area and CDs have electrical conductivity, which were benefited to build the composite modified electrode. The three dihydroxybenzene isomers could well separate from each other at the scan rate of 100mVs−1 with a potential difference of 119mV and 419mV for the oxidation peak potentials of HQ–CC and CC–RS, respectively. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was used for the simultaneous determination of HQ, CC and RS in their ternary mixture. The peak current obtained was linearly dependent on the HQ, CC and RS concentrations in the range of 1.0 to 200.0μM, 4.0 to 200.0μM and 3.0 to 400.0μM and the detection limits for HQ, CC and RS were 0.07μM, 0.06μM and 0.15μM (S/N=3), respectively. The modified electrode was applied to detect the tap water, well water and river water and the recoveries of tap water, well water and river water samples were 100.3–109.8% for HQ, 96.0–105.8% for CC and 83.4–101.6% for RS, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: simultaneous determination
dihydroxybenzene isomers
electrochemical sensor
carbon dots
multi-walled carbon nanotubes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs, functionalization of MWCNTs
Characterization of MWCNTs, CDs, MWCNTs/CDs and MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs/GCE
Cyclic voltammetric response of Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs to HQ, CC and RS
Simultaneous determination of HQ, CC and RS

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The reaction mechanism of electrostatic interaction between MWCNTs and CDs.
TEM image of CDs (A) and SEM image of raw MWCNTs (B), acidized MWCNTs (C) and amination MWCNTs.
AFM image of the bare GCE (A), Nafion/GCE (B), Nafion/MWCNTs (C). Nafion/CDs (D), Nafion/MWCNTs-CDs (E), Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs (F).

CV of 0.10mM HQ, CC and RS recorded on bare GCE, Nafion/GCE, Nafion/CDs, Nafion/MWCNTs, Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs and Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs in the pH 7.0 phosphate buffer solution at the scan rate of 0.1Vs−1. The insets showed the comparesion of the bare GCE, Nafion/GCE, Nafion/CDs.

(A) Effect of scan rate on the redox behavior of HQ (0.1mM), CC (0.1mM) and RS (0.1mM) in 0.1M PBS (pH=7.0) at Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs (a-i: 0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25, 0.3, 0.35, 0.4, 0.45, 0.5V s−1) (B) plots of peak current versus scan rates 1/2 (v: 0.1– 0.5V s−1) for HQ, CC, and RS.

(A) Effect of pH on the redox behavior of HQ (0.1mM), CC (0.1mM) and RS (0.1mM) in 0.1M PBS (pH=7.0) at Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs (a-i: 4.0, 4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 7.5, 8.0) (B) plots of peak potential versus pH (v: 0.01– 0.5V s−1) for HQ, CC and RS.

(A) DPVs of Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs in the presence of 50.0μmolL−1 CC and 50.0μmolL−1 RS, containing different concentrations of HQ. Inset: calibration plots of HQ. (a–j: 1.0, 2.0, 4.0, 6.0, 10.0, 30.0, 50.0, 70.0, 100.0, 200.0μmolL−1) (B) DPVs of Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs in the presence of 50.0μmolL−1 HQ and RS, containing different concentrations of CC. Inset: calibration plots of CC. (a–h: 4.0, 6.0, 10.0, 30.0, 50.0, 70.0, 100.0, 200.0μmolL−1) (C) DPVs of Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs in the presence of 50.0μmolL−1 CC and 50.0μmolL−1 HQ, containing different concentrations of RS. Inset: calibration plots of RS. (a–k: 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 10.0, 30.0, 50.0, 70.0, 100.0, 200.0, 300.0, 400.0μmolL−1) (D) Simultaneously increasing concentration of HQ, CC and RS from 20μM to 120μM.
The stability of repetitive measurements of DPV response for HQ (0.1mM), CC (0.1mM) and RS (0.1mM) in 0.1M PBS (pH=7.0) at Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs.
Performance comparison of the fabricated electrode for HQ, CC and RS detection with other electrodes.
Recoveries of HQ, CC and RS in tap water, well water and river water samples at the Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs electrode.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous electrochemical determination of hydroquinone, catechol and resorcinol at Nafion/multi-walled carbon nanotubes/carbon dots/multi-walled carbon nanotubes modified glassy carbon electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food and Biological Engineering, Zhangzhou Institute of Technology, Zhangzhou 363000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhangzhou Products Quality Supervision Institute, Zhangzhou 363000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, an excellent electrochemical sensor based on Nafion/multi-walled carbon nanotubes/carbon dots/multi-walled carbon nanotubes (Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs) for simultaneous determination of hydroquinone (HQ), catechol (CC) and resorcinol (RS) in 0.1M phosphate buffer solutions (PBS, pH=7.0) was found. The functioned MWCNTs, which contained amido bond, were combined to CDs via the electrostatic interaction to build the Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs modified electrode. As we know, MWCNTs have large specific surface area and CDs have electrical conductivity, which were benefited to build the composite modified electrode. The three dihydroxybenzene isomers could well separate from each other at the scan rate of 100mVs−1 with a potential difference of 119mV and 419mV for the oxidation peak potentials of HQ–CC and CC–RS, respectively. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was used for the simultaneous determination of HQ, CC and RS in their ternary mixture. The peak current obtained was linearly dependent on the HQ, CC and RS concentrations in the range of 1.0 to 200.0μM, 4.0 to 200.0μM and 3.0 to 400.0μM and the detection limits for HQ, CC and RS were 0.07μM, 0.06μM and 0.15μM (S/N=3), respectively. The modified electrode was applied to detect the tap water, well water and river water and the recoveries of tap water, well water and river water samples were 100.3–109.8% for HQ, 96.0–105.8% for CC and 83.4–101.6% for RS, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: The dihydroxybenzene isomers, hydroquinone (HQ), catechol (CC) and resorcinol (RS) existed in environment as the chemicals of polluted waters. They could enter into the human body through the skin, respiratory and digestive apparatus to erode skin and mucosa, and even inhibit nerve center system [1]. In addition to the negative effect mentioned above, hydroquinone (HQ) with high concentration could incur headache, fatigue, tachycardia, decompensation, the damage to kidney and even death, catechol (CC) could induce DNA damage and cause human carcinogen [1,2]. For their genotoxicity, carcinogenicity and toxicokinetic effect on human and their low degradation in environment, they have been included in the lists of priority pollutants by international bodies such as the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the European Union (EU) [3]. The environmental quality standards for surface water in the national standard of China (GB 3838 - 2002) was 0.005mgL−1. So far, many detection methods have been found to detect these isomers, including spectrophotometry [4], fluorescence [5], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6] and electrochemical methods [7–11]. Nowadays, with the advantages of high efficiency and sensitivity, wide linear analysis range, simplicity, low cost and time saving, environment electrochemical methods have became an important investigation domain [12]. Unfortunately, the oxidation and reduction of the dihydroxybenzene isomers are always overlapped at the unmodified electrodes. And there are still few articles that were detect the three isomers, HQ, CC and RS in the meantime.

As one kind of nano-materials, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) was discovered by Iijima in 1991 [13]. Due to their unique dimensional structure with the strong π-π conjugate action, CNTs have efficient electron transfer efficiency. CNTs were applied in electrochemistry as the electrode modification material that fixed on the electrode surface to detect the dihydroxybenzene isomers simultaneously. In order to obtain the stronger signal and well-defined peaks, many composite chemicals have been modified to the bare electrode for simultaneous determination of the dihydroxybenzene isomers. For examples, the electrodes which were modified with the three-dimensional graphene/MWCNTs/1-butyl-3-methylimidazoliun hexafluorophosphate (BMIMPF6) nanocomposite [9], the graphene oxide (GO)-terthiophene-CNT hybrid material [10], or the polymeric ionic liquid (PIL)-MWCNTs [11], etc, have been prepared. However, the procedures of the preparation were intricate and time consuming, and the organic materials they used were polluted our environment with toxicity. On the other hand, because of their superiority in water solubility, chemical inertness, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and versatile surface chemistry, carbon dots (CDs) have been used in biological sensors, medical imaging equipment and tiny light-emitting diodes [14–16]. And CDs are of two distinctive features: one is that the underlying carbon particles are very small, and the other is that the particle surface is passivated by organic or other molecules via either covalent linkages or chemical adsorption [17].

In this work, the modified electrodes that based on the Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs were used to simultaneously detect the HQ, CC and RS with stability and sensitivity. And there were numerous carboxyl groups on CDs' surface for the preparation process through microwave-mediated caramelization of aqueous [18,19]. So after functionalization, the MWCNTs which contain lots of amidogen were easy to connect to CDs by electrostatic interaction [20]. The composites were soluble in water, so dropping few Nafion on the electrode's surface could protect the modified electrode. The peak current was linearly dependent on the HQ, CC and RS concentrations in the range of 1.0 to 200.0μM, 4.0 to 200.0μM and 3.0 to 400.0μM respectively. The detection limit of HQ, CC and RS were 0.07μM, 0.06μM and 0.15μM (S/N=3) respectively. In addition, this methods was applied to the detection of HQ, CC and RS in the tap water, well water and river water with satisfactory results. The recoveries of tap water, well water and river water samples were 100.3–109.8% for HQ, 96.0–105.8% for CC and 83.4–1 01.6% for RS respectively.

CC, RS was brought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Shanghai Co., Ltd (China). HQ was purchased from DA hao fine chemicals co., LTD (Shantou, Guangdong, China). MWCNTs was purchased from Shenzhen Nanotech Port Co., Ltd. Nitric acid, sulfuric acid, ethylenediamine and polyethylene glycol-200 were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., LTD (Guangdong, China). Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) (pH=7.0) were prepared by mixing stock standard solutions of Na2HPO4–12H2O (0.1molL−1) and NaH2PO4–2H2O (0.1molL−1). And the perfluorinated resin solution (Nafion) was purchased from Sigma-aldrich, co., (USA). All chemicals were prepared with doubly distiller water.

The functionalization of MWCNTs was with the help of a WH – 200 Ultrasonic cleaning machine. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were carried out on a CHI 650D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chen Hua Instruments Co., China) with a three-electrode system that the modified electrode to be the working electrode, a platinum electrode to be the counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl/saturated KCl to be the reference electrode. All the CV and DPV analysis were take in 10mL of 0.1mL−1 PBS (pH=7) at room temperature. And the experiment also need a microwave oven (Galanz) and an AE240 electronic analytical balance (Shanghai Mettler-Toledo Instruments Co., Ltd).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs, functionalization of MWCNTs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were prepared by microwave. 2.0000g glucose was dissolve in 3mL distiller water and then 10.00mL polyethylene glycol-200 (PEG–200) was added in. The mixtures were put in a 540W microwave oven to obtain a yellow CDs solution after three minutes [19]. After that, the CDs were dialyzed for 24h with the dialysis membranes of 1000 cutoffs, then stored at 4°C ready for use.

The MWCNTs were purify by means of reflux in the mixture solution of nitric acid and sulfuric acid (1:3, v/v) for 2h in 140°C in order to acquire the water solubility and more carboxyl [21]. After cooling down to room temperature, the mixtures were filtered with a cellulose membrane filter (pore size 0.25μm) and washed several times with distilled water until the pH nearly 7 [20,21]. The resulting black solid was reacted with ethylenediamine for 24h via ultrasonic. At last, the functional MWCNTs were dialyzed for 24h in order to remove the redundant ethylenediamine. In addition, the functionalization of MWCNTs was stereo omni-directional which made contribution to connect with CDs and MWCNTs.

The bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was firstly polished with 1.0μm, 0.3μm, 0.05μm in turn, and then washed with distill water, ethanol and distilled water in an ultrasonic bath, blow with nitrogen at last.

Synthesis of Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs modified electrode: As the Fig. 1 shown, for the reference of the layer by layer (LBL) techniques, 5μL of the functional MWCNTs, 5μL of CDs solution and 5μL of the functional MWCNTs were dropped on the GCE orderly. CDs, as the negative ion, were reacted with the positive ion MWCNTs for the electrostatic interaction [20]. The last step was added 1μL Nafion on the surface of modified electrode in order to protect the modifier electrode from dissolution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of MWCNTs, CDs, MWCNTs/CDs and MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 (A-D) showed the morphology of CDs by transmission electron microscope (TEM), and raw MWCNTs and functionalized MWCNTs by Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM). In comparison, the MWCNTs of amination were easier to reunite than the MWCNTs of acidification. AFM images of the bare GCE, Nafion/GCE, Nafion/MWCNTs/GCE, Nafion/CDs/GCE, Nafion/MWCNTs-CDs/GCE, Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs/GCE were shown in Fig. 3 (A-F) and Fig. S1(A-F), which were used to explain the structure of the modified electrode. The average roughness were 0.228nm, 0.397nm, 2.040nm, 0.582nm, 2.630nm, 8.580nm of the bare GCE, Nafion/GCE, Nafion/MWCNTs/GCE, Nafion/CDs/GCE, Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/GCE, Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs/GCE respectively. With increasing the layer, the average roughness were increased. As show in Fig. S2(A-F), the largest peaks height were 0.393nm, 0.520nm, 20.011nm, 0.847nm, 30.670nm, 79.360nm of bare GCE, Nafion/GCE, Nafion/MWCNTs/GCE, Nafion/CDs/GCE, Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/GCE, Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs/GCE respectively. These data proved the succes of connectiong CDs with MWCNTs and modifying the electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic voltammetric response of Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs to HQ, CC and RS
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical responses of HQ, CC and RS at bare GCE, Nafion/GCE, Nafion/CDs/GCE, Nafion/MWCNTs/GCE, Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/GCE and Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs/GCE were examined by CV in the 0.1M PBS (pH=7.0) containing 0.1mM HQ, 0.1mM CC and 0.1mM RS at the scan rate of 0.1Vs−1 respectively. With the ability to promote electron-transfer process and high specific surface area, MWCNTs were present excellent electrochemical properties. CDs were easily conjugated to MWCNTs because of their chemical surface. As shown in Fig. 4 , the signal of the Nation/GCE electrode was weaken dramatically of its poorly electrical conductivity. There were only two pair redox peaks nearly at 0.334V and 0.678V of bare GCE, Nafion/GCE and Nafion/CDs electrode, and the oxidation waves of HQ and CC were overlapped. When using the Nafion/MWCNTs/GCE, Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/GCE or Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs/GCE, three independently well-define oxidation peaks were clearly observed, which manifested that the three isomers could be separated entirely. At Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs/GCE, the peak potential of the oxidation peaks of HQ, CC and RS were at 129mV, 248mV and 667mV, respectively. The anodic (ΔEpa1) potential differences between HQ and CC of Nafion/MWCNTs/GCE, Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/GCE and Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs/GCE were 118mV, 116mV and 119mV, respectively. And the anodic (ΔEpa2) potential differences between CC and RS were 455mV, 424mV and 419mV, respectively. Which indicated that the Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs/GCE for simultaneous determination of HQ, CC and RS had higher sensitivity than other electrodes. Apart from distinguish of the three isomers clearly, the electrochemical signal was enhanced significantly at Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs modified electrode. As shown in the Fig. S3, the electrochemical sign of Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs/GCE was higher than Nafion/MWCNTs/MWCNTs/GCE, which proved the role of CDs. In addition, the comparison of each electrode by DPV was shown in Fig. S4 in the 0.1M PBS (pH=7.0) containing 0.1mM HQ, 0.1mM CC and 0.1mM RS at the scan rate of 0.1Vs−1. The intensity of electrochemical signal by DPV was consistent with the signal by CV.

The influence of scan rate on the redox of HQ, CC and RS was investigated at the Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs electrode as show in Fig. 5 A. The redox peaks current increased gradually with the increase of scan rate. The redox peak currents of HQ, CC and RS increased linearly with the square root of scan rate in the range of 0.1–0.5Vs−1. As shown in Fig. 5B, the regression equation of HQ, CC and RS were Ipc (μA)=-0.5285 υ1/2 (υ in mVs−1)+2.6788 (R=0.9956), Ipc (μA)=-0.5790 υ1/2 +2.9544 (R=0.9960) and Ipc (μA)=-0.2046 υ1/2 +0.9768 (R=0.9910), respectively. The slopes were closed to 0.5, which could indicate the electro-redox of HQ, CC and RS at Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs modified electrode for a typical diffusion-controlled process.

Fig. 6 shows the effect of pH on the Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs electrode of 0.1mM HQ, 0.1mM CC and 0.1mM RS in 0.1M PBS (pH=7) at the range of 4.0–8.0. With increasing the pH, the peak currents of HQ, CC and RS increased, and the potentials shifted negatively due to the participation of protons in the electrode reaction. The potentials of HQ, CC and RS followed the linear regression equations with pH: Epa (V)=-0.0569 pH+0.5034 (R=0.9988), Epa (V)=-0.0576 pH+0.6325 (R=0.9973) and Epa (V)=-0.0593 pH+1.0356 (R=0.9841) respectively. According to the formula, dEp/dpH=2.303 mRT/nF, where m and n are the number of proton and electron respectively. The slope of the three regression equation is close to the theoretical value of 0.0585V pH−1, indicating that the electrochemical redox of HQ, CC and RS should be a two electrons and two proton process [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous determination of HQ, CC and RS
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate the determination sensitivity, DPVwas used to record the oxidation peak currents of HQ, CC and RS at Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs electrode. As Fig. 7 shown, DPV was adopted to investigate HQ, CC and RS within different concentration of HQ, CC and RS, respectively. In Fig. 7(A-C), when detecting the individual, the concentration of one substance was changed with the others remained constant [11], and in Fig. 7D, DPV of increasing the concentration of HQ, CC and RS simultaneously from 20μM to 120μM was used. Keeping the concentration of CC and RS both at 50.0μM, the peak current increased proportionally with adding concentrations of HQ from 1.0 to 200.0μM. A linear regression equation between the oxidation peak current with the concentration of HQ was obtained: Ipc =-0.0502 c - 0.4346 (Ipc in μA, c in μmoL−1) (R=0.9994). The detection limit for HQ was calculated to be 0.07μM (S/N=3). Similarly, to keep the concentration of HQ and RS both at 50.0μM, the concentration of CC was changed within the scope of 4.0 to 200.0μM. And the linear regression equation of CC was got: Ipc =-0.0568 c - 0.1602 (Ipc in μA, c in μmoL−1) (R=0.9933). The detection limit for CC was calculated to be 0.06μM (S/N=3). In the same way, the concentration of HQ and CC remained were kept at 50.0μM and the concentration range of RS was from 3.0 to 400.0μM, the linear regression equation of RS was: Ipc =-0.0259 c - 0.0805 and Ipc =-0.0065 c–1.466 (Ipc in μA, c in μmoL−1) (R=0.9901) and the detection limit for CC was 0.15μM (S/N=3). The error bars of each insets in Fig. 7 were below 5%.

According to Fig. 7 the Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs modified electrode enhanced the corresponding peak current and exhibited improved catalytic separation performance of the coexisting of HQ, CC and RS. The density of electron cloud of HQ molecule is higher than that of CC and RS, leading to a decrease of oxidation activity at electrode surface for the latter [11]. Owing to MWCNTs and CDs' unique characteristic, the composite owned huge specific surface, efficient electron transfer efficiency and porous structure, which could catalyze the oxidation of HQ, CC and RS and result in the separation of oxidation peaks [11,23].

It is very important to detect the selectivity in analyzing the real samples. As a general rule, if the relative error in the determination was controlled at approximately ±5% that it could be considered to have no interference. The interference of some common ions in determination of HQ (0.1mM), CC (0.1mM), RS (0.1mM) including Na+, Cl−, Zn2+, SO4 2−, Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, NO3 − (each of 0.1M) was investigated in PBS (pH=7) at the modified electrode. The variation in current caused by the interference ion was less than 5% that indicated an excellent selectivity of Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs modified electrode.

To evaluate the practical application of Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs modified electrode for water sample analysis, three kinds of water samples, the tap water, well water and river water were detected after filtering with a cellulose membrane filter (pore size 0.25μm) for several times. The calibration samples were analyzed by adding the concentration of determinand sample by sample with DPV. The comparison of the modified electrode with other modified electrodes was listed in Table 1 . As show in Table 2 , the recoveries of three water samples were 100.3–109.8% for HQ, 96.0–105.8% for CC and 83.4–101.6% for RS respectively, indicating practical applicability and reliability of Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs/GCE for simultaneous determination of HQ, CC and RS in practice water samples.

The stability and reproducibility of the modified electrode were investigated. DPV response for 0.1mM HQ, 0.1 mM CC and 0.1mM RS in 0.1 M PBS (pH=7) at MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs electrode was shown in Fig. 8 . The relative standard deviation (RSD) for HQ, CC and RS were 0.51%, 1.07% and 2.15%, respectively. Therefore, Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs electrode has an excellent stability and reproducibility.

In this subject, a sensitive and reliable electrochemical platform was designed to detect the electrochemical redox of HQ, CC and RS at Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs modified electrode. CDs were connected to the amination MWCNTs via the electrostatic interaction. The influence of scan rate, pH and usual ion were explored of HQ, CC and RS in 0.1M PBS. For simultaneous determination of HQ, CC and RS, three pairs of independently well-define quasi-reversible redox peaks was obtained. And the Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs/GCE exhibited a low detection limit and wide linear range, and the detection limits for HQ, CC, and RS were 0.07μM, 0.06μM, 0.15μM (S/N=3) respectively. The recoveries of tap water, well water and river water samples were 100.3–109.8% for HQ, 96.0–105.8% for CC and 83.4–101.6% for RS respectively, indicating practical applicability and reliability of Nafion/MWCNTs/CDs/MWCNTs/GCE for determination of HQ, CC and RS in practice water samples simultaneously.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the science and technology foundation of the national general administration of quality supervision in China (NO. 2012QK053), Fujian province natural science foundation (NO. 2012D136), the education bureau of Fujian province of China (No. JB14180), the science and technology foundation of Fujian provincial bureau quality and technical supervision (NO. FJQI2012029, NO.FJQI 2013108).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2014.10.051.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrocatalytic redox of hydroquinone by two forms of l-Proline
Catechol and hydroquinone have different redox properties responsible for their differential DNA-damaging ability
Simultaneous determination of positional isomers of benzenediols by capillary zone electrophoresis with square wave amperometric detection
A sensitive and selective spectrophotometric estimation of catechol derivatives in pharmaceutical preparations
Determination of phenol, resorcinol and hydroquinone in air samples by synchronous fluorescence using partial least-squares (PLS)
Direct analysis of phenol, catechol and hydroquinone in human urine by coupled-column HPLC with fluorimetric detection
Electrochemical behavior of catechol, resorcinol and hydroquinone at graphene–chitosan composite film modified glassy carbon electrode and their simultaneous determination in water samples
Highly stable pyridinic nitrogen doped grapheme modified electrode in simultaneous determination of hydroquinone and catechol
Simultaneous electrochemical determination of hydroquinone and catechol based on three-dimensional graphene/MWCNTs/BMIMPF6 nanocomposite modified electrode
Electrochemical sensor for hydroquinone and catechol based on electrochemically reduced GO–terthiophene–CNT
Discrimination and simultaneous determination of hydroquinone and catechol by tunable polymerization of imidazolium-based ionic liquid on multi-walled carbon nanotube surfaces
A hydroquinone sensor based on a new nanocrystals modified electrode
